perturbation.original_text: 2.2 MSCI Grant. MSCI hereby grants (subject to any existing third party contractual obligations) to MS a non-exclusive, perpetual, irrevocable, world- wide, royalty-free license for MS to use, modify, copy, create derivative works of and sublicense, for any business purpose, the MSCI Licensed Materials.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2 MSCI Grant. MSCI hereby grants (subject to any existing third party contractual obligations) to MS a non-exclusive, perpetual, revocable, world- wide, royalty-free license for MS to use, modify, copy, create derivative works of and sublicense, for any business purpose, the MSCI Licensed Materials.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'irrevocable' to 'revocable' fundamentally alters the nature of the license grant. An irrevocable license is permanent, whereas a revocable license can be terminated. This contradiction creates uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under New York contract law, a license, especially when tied to consideration, is generally considered irrevocable unless otherwise stated, particularly when it affects property rights. Changing 'irrevocable' to 'revocable' makes the license's enforceability questionable and contradicts standard interpretations of perpetual licenses.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The change from 'irrevocable' to 'revocable' directly contradicts the nature of a perpetual license. The Perturbation Explanation states that an irrevocable license is permanent, while a revocable license can be terminated, creating uncertainty. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that licenses tied to consideration are generally considered irrevocable unless otherwise stated, especially when affecting property rights. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the change fundamentally alters the agreement from a permanent grant to one that can be terminated, directly contradicting the concept of a perpetual license and rendering it unenforceable as originally intended.

perturbation.original_text: 4. NO WARRANTIES THE LICENSE GRANTS HEREUNDER ARE PROVIDED "AS-IS" WITH NO WARRANTIES, AND THE PARTIES EXPRESSLY EXCLUDE AND DISCLAIM ANY WARRANTIES UNDER OR ARISING AS A RESULT OF THIS AGREEMENT, WHETHER EXPRESS, IMPLIED OR STATUTORY, INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, THE IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, TITLE, NON-INFRINGEMENT OR ANY OTHER WARRANTY WHATSOEVER.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. NO WARRANTIES THE LICENSE GRANTS HEREUNDER ARE PROVIDED "AS-IS" WITH NO WARRANTIES, AND THE PARTIES EXPRESSLY EXCLUDE AND DISCLAIM ANY WARRANTIES UNDER OR ARISING AS A RESULT OF THIS AGREEMENT, WHETHER EXPRESS, IMPLIED OR STATUTORY, EXCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, THE IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, TITLE, NON-INFRINGEMENT OR ANY OTHER WARRANTY WHATSOEVER.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'including' to 'excluding' in the disclaimer of warranties creates a contradiction. The original text disclaims all warranties; the modified text appears to affirm them, leading to ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-316 governs the exclusion or modification of warranties in contracts for the sale of goods. While 'as is' clauses can disclaim warranties, contradicting the disclaimer by excluding the very warranties you intend to disclaim creates ambiguity that can render the disclaimer ineffective, contradicting the intent of the clause under the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The change from 'including' to 'excluding' fundamentally alters the meaning of the warranty disclaimer. The original text disclaims all warranties, including specific ones. The modified text attempts to exclude all warranties, but then excludes the very warranties it intends to disclaim creating ambiguity, making the disclaimer ineffective. UCC § 2-316 addresses the exclusion of warranties. Snippet 1 states that language that negates or limits a warranty should be construed as consistent, but that any negation is inoperative to the extent such construction is unreasonable. Changing 'including' to 'excluding' creates an unreasonable and contradictory construction, because it is not reasonable to include and exclude the same warranties at the same time. The 'as is' clause does not overcome the fundamental contradiction created by attempting to 'exclude' warranties one is trying to exclude.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 Governing Law; Jurisdiction; Dispute Resolution. (a) This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with and governed by the substantive internal laws of the State of New York. MSCI Inc. is registered to do business in New York under the name NY MSCI.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 Governing Law; Jurisdiction; Dispute Resolution. (a) This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with and governed by the procedural laws of the State of New York. MSCI Inc. is registered to do business in New York under the name NY MSCI.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'substantive internal laws' to 'procedural laws' drastically alters the framework for interpreting the contract. Substantive laws define rights and duties, while procedural laws govern how legal proceedings are conducted. This creates a contradiction regarding which body of law dictates the contract's interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 addresses choice of law in contract cases. Specifying 'procedural' law is unusual because contracts are typically governed by the 'substantive' law of the chosen jurisdiction. This modification could lead to a situation where New York procedural rules are applied to a contract whose underlying rights and obligations are undefined, directly contradicting standard conflict of laws principles and creating an unenforceable agreement due to lack of clarity.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Penn WebLogin Log in with your PennKey Username Password Log in Forgot username? Forgot password? Change my password Test my PennKey Privacy policy
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies adherence to 'substantive internal laws,' which define rights and duties. The change to 'procedural laws' alters the framework for interpreting the contract, focusing instead on the rules for conducting legal proceedings. According to the Law Explanation, this deviates from standard conflict of laws principles where contracts are governed by 'substantive' law. The change contradicts legal standards by potentially creating an unenforceable agreement. The fact that the scraped snippets are not applicable does not detract from the contradiction identified based on the explanation and definitions provided.
